actions.thought: 2h 4d is an extremely weak starting hand with very low equity and poor playability. Attempting to play this hand from the button would be a negative expected value decision. Folding is the clear and standard play to preserve the stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 3c 9h, which is an extremely weak starting hand. The Button has already folded, so it is just me and the Big Blind. Completing the small blind or raising with 93o is generally unprofitable as I will be out of position for the rest of the hand with poor equity. The best play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with 95s. In a 3-handed game, the button is the most advantageous position. However, 95s is a marginal hand that sits at the bottom of an opening range. Given it is early in the tournament (Hand #2), I will play conservatively and fold this hand to observe the tendencies of the Small Blind and Big Blind before committing chips with mediocre holdings.

I have 74o in the small blind after the button folded. This hand is incredibly weak, completely disconnected and unsuited. Even though it's a blind vs blind situation where I can open wider, 74o is well outside any reasonable opening range, and playing out of position post-flop with garbage is a losing proposition. Folding is the clear choice.

K9 suited is a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. Being on the button, I want to take the initiative and raise to steal the blinds or play a heads-up pot in position. A standard raise to 2.5x the big blind (250) is optimal to accomplish this while maintaining pot control.

With 4c3c in the small blind, I am facing a raise from the button. This hand is generally too weak to play out of position against an aggressive button range. Given the early stage of the tournament, I prefer to fold and wait for a more advantageous spot to enter the pot, rather than engaging in a potentially losing battle out of position.

I am in the Big Blind with 9c 6d. The Button (Pro) has raised to 250. It costs me 150 to call into a pot of 400. While the pot odds are roughly 2.6:1, 9-6 offsuit is a very weak hand to play out of position against a player designated as a 'Pro'. In a 150-hand tournament, I should avoid marginal spots where I am likely to be dominated or forced to play a weak hand post-flop without the initiative. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Ten-Seven offsuit is a very weak starting hand with low board connectivity and poor equity against the likely ranges of the Small Blind and Big Blind. Even with the positional advantage of the button, the risk of investing chips to see a flop with this hand is not justified. Given we are early in the tournament and maintaining a healthy stack is critical, folding is the most disciplined and long-term profitable decision.

I have As5s in the small blind, which is a very strong hand for a head-up situation against the big blind after the button folds. Raising is the standard play here to put pressure on the BB and capitalize on the hand's equity and playability. A 3x raise to 300 is a standard and effective sizing.
93o is a very weak starting hand. Facing a 3x raise from the small blind, this is an easy fold to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 2c 3d, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. While being on the button offers a good opportunity to steal the blinds, this hand has almost no post-flop equity or playability. Given it is early in the tournament and I just successfully stole the blinds in the previous hand, there is no need to risk chips with such a low-quality hand. Folding is the most prudent and standard play here.

J7 suited is a good candidate for a steal/raise blind vs blind. It has decent playability if called, and raising puts maximum pressure on the Big Blind. I'll make it 3x to build the pot or take down the dead money.

6-10 offsuit is a weak hand with poor playability, especially when out of position against a raise from the small blind. Defending the big blind with this holding against a 3x raise is mathematically negative expected value, as I will frequently be in a difficult spot post-flop. It is better to conserve my stack for a more advantageous situation.

KQo is a strong starting hand, especially when playing 3-handed. From the Button, it is important to play aggressively and open with a raise. A raise size of 2.5x the big blind (250 chips) is standard to deter the blinds from calling with marginal hands while building a pot when we are in position.

I am in the small blind with an Ace-Deuce offsuit. Given the button's raise, calling is a standard defense strategy as I have decent equity and an Ace blocker. Folding would be too conservative, and 3-betting is not ideal with this holding out of position. I will call to see a flop and re-evaluate.

I am in the Big Blind with Q9o. The Button (Pro) raised to 250 and the Small Blind (Flashlight) called. I need to call 150 more to see a flop. The pot is currently 600, giving me 4:1 odds on a call. Q9o is a strong enough hand to defend in this spot against a button opening range and a small blind call, especially with the excellent price provided. I will call and evaluate the flop.

The flop is highly coordinated with three diamonds, and my Ace-high hand has no equity. With two opponents, any significant bet from them will force me to fold. Checking is the only prudent option here to preserve my stack, as I have no piece of this board and no draw to speak of.

I have middle pair (9s) on a very wet board (monotone diamonds). However, I also hold the Queen of Diamonds (Qd), giving me a strong flush draw and a gutshot straight draw. Flashlight checked to me. As the Big Blind, checking is the most prudent play here to see how the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) acts. My hand has too much value to fold but isn't strong enough to lead out into two opponents on such a dangerous board. Checking allows me to realize my equity or potentially catch a bluff.

We completely missed this highly coordinated monotone flop (Jd 9d 8d) and hold no diamond in our hand. With two opponents, it is very likely that at least one of them has connected with this board, either with a made hand, a strong draw, or a high diamond. A continuation bet here would be a pure bluff with almost zero equity when called. Checking behind to take a free card and realize what little equity we have is the most prudent play.

I am first to act on the turn with Ace-high and no pair or draw. The board is quite wet with three diamonds. Since all three players checked the flop, checking is the safest and most logical play. Betting here would be a pure bluff with almost no equity, and it is unlikely to force out better hands that checked the flop. I will check to keep the pot controlled and re-evaluate on the river.

The turn 4s is a total brick. Everyone checked the flop, which indicates that nobody likely has a made flush or a very strong hand like a set or straight. Flashlight has checked again from the Small Blind. I have middle pair (9s) and a strong Queen-high flush draw. Checking here is the best play to control the pot size and realize my equity. If the Button (Pro) bets, I have an easy call; if it checks around again, I get a free look at the river with a hand that still has decent showdown value and great drawing potential.

The turn brings a blank 4s. Both opponents have checked again, showing weakness or a tricky draw. However, our hand (KQ high, no diamonds) still has very little equity and bluffing into two opponents on a monotone, highly coordinated board is risky. Checking behind again is the safest option. We might hit a King, Queen, or a non-diamond Ten on the river, or just get to showdown for free if it checks down.

I have reached the river with only Ace-high and no pair. The board is quite connected, and given that all three players have checked through the flop and turn, the chances that someone has a pair are extremely high. Bluffing into two opponents after they have shown weakness is unlikely to succeed, and checking is the standard play to minimize losses with a hand that has zero showdown value. Betting here would be a pure bluff with little chance of forcing folds from any hand that can beat me, which is almost any pair.

On the river, I have improved to two pair (Queens and Nines). The board is extremely dangerous with three diamonds and potential straights (any Ten makes a straight), but the action has been exceptionally passive with everyone checking the flop and turn. Since Flashlight has checked for the third time and the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) also checked twice, it is unlikely anyone is sitting on a strong flush or a high straight. However, my two pair is still vulnerable and betting might only get called by better hands while folding out hands I currently beat. Checking allows me to see a showdown for free or react to Pro's final action. I will check to realize my showdown value.

The river gives us top pair, but the board is extremely dangerous with Jd-9d-8d-4s-Qs. Any Ten makes a straight, and any diamond makes a flush. Both opponents have checked to us three times, meaning they likely don't have a monster, but betting our top pair for value is far too thin. We are unlikely to be called by worse hands, and we open ourselves up to being check-raised by a disguised trap or a newly formed straight. Taking a free showdown is the optimal play here to realize our showdown value.

My starting hand, 9-5 offsuit, is extremely weak and lacks sufficient equity to warrant an investment. Even in the button position, it is standard to fold junk hands like this to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with Qh 2s. The Button (Flashlight) has folded. Queen-Deuce offsuit is a very weak starting hand with poor playability, especially when out of position against the Big Blind (Pro), who has shown a tendency to be aggressive in previous hands. While the cost to call is only 50 chips, the expected value of playing this hand out of position is negative. Therefore, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with 6h4s, which is a very weak starting hand with low equity and poor playability. In a three-handed game, while ranges are wider, 64-offsuit is still well below the threshold for a profitable opening raise or call. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

85o is a very weak hand. Even though I am in the small blind and it only costs 50 more chips to limp, playing this garbage hand out of position against the big blind is unprofitable. The best decision is to fold and preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I have 23 offsuit, which is widely considered the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. It has no high card strength, no flush potential, and its straight potential is extremely weak. Even from the Button, it is an easy fold.

I am in the small blind and the button has folded, leaving only the big blind to act. King-Jack offsuit is a strong holding in a heads-up situation, and raising allows me to apply pressure and potentially win the pot pre-flop or take control of the hand. A raise to 300 is a standard size to discourage the big blind while building a pot with a premium-adjacent hand.

I have Ace-Deuce of hearts in the Big Blind. The Small Blind (Flashlight) raised to 300. With a suited Ace, this is a very strong hand in a blind-vs-blind scenario. The pot odds are 2:1 (200 to call into a 400 pot), and my hand has excellent playability post-flop. I will call to see the flop and keep the pot size controlled while retaining the ability to hit a nut flush or an Ace.

The board is extremely coordinated with three hearts, hitting the big blind's defending range significantly. I hold the Jack of Hearts, giving me a strong flush draw and two overcards. While checking is a viable defensive option out of position, betting 300 into the 600 pot maintains my aggression from pre-flop and forces my opponent to potentially fold non-heart hands, allowing me to take down the pot or continue with equity on the turn.

I have the nut flush on a 4h 6h 7h flop. This is an exceptionally strong position. While slow-playing is an option, the board is very 'wet' and many turn cards could either kill the action or complicate the board (any fourth heart or a board pair). By raising now, I build the pot immediately against Flashlight's likely range of sets, lower flushes, or strong one-heart hands like the Kh or Qh. A raise to 900 puts pressure on their range while setting up a potential stack-off on later streets.

The opponent has raised on a monotone board, which is a polarized move that could represent a made flush or a strong made hand trying to protect against a heart draw. Holding the Jack of Hearts gives me some equity and blocks some combinations, though it is not the nut flush draw. With the current pot odds, calling 600 to see the turn is the most disciplined approach, allowing me to evaluate further on the next card without overcommitting in a potentially dominated situation.

The board texture is highly coordinated with three hearts. I have top pair (Kings) with the Jack of Hearts, which serves as a blocker to the nut flush, but I am still vulnerable to any made flush. Given that my opponent raised the flop, they have shown strength and could easily be holding a made flush, a straight, or a smaller made hand. Checking here allows me to keep the pot size manageable and evaluate my opponent's action. If they bet, I can better assess the pot odds against my top pair; if they check behind, I get to see a river card for free, which is ideal given the dangerous nature of the board.

I have the nut flush (Ace-high flush) on a board that is not paired, meaning I currently hold the unbeatable hand. The King of Diamonds on the turn is mostly a blank card, though it could potentially provide a second-best hand for Flashlight if they held a King with a heart. After Flashlight checked to me, I need to continue building the pot to extract maximum value from their likely holding of a set, two-pair, or a lower flush. A bet of 1600 into a 2400 pot (two-thirds pot) is a strong value sizing that sets up a potential all-in on the river while remaining small enough to be called by a wide range of made hands.

The board is monotone, and the opponent has shown significant strength by raising the flop and continuing to bet on the turn. My hand, Top Pair (Kings) with a Jack of Hearts, is currently a bluff-catcher at best. Given the board texture, the opponent likely has a made flush, a straight, or a set, all of which crush my equity. While I hold the Jack of Hearts which blocks some of the nut flush combinations, calling is mathematically incorrect as I likely have less than 20% equity against their range, and I am not getting the required pot odds to justify a call. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities.

My holding of Jack-three offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability, especially at a table with 3 players. There is no strategic justification for investing chips into the pot with this range from the button, so I will fold.

57o is a weak hand to play from the Small Blind. Although the price to call is only 50 chips, I would be out of position against Pro for the rest of the hand. Given my current chip lead and the early stage of the tournament, I'd rather wait for a better spot than defend a marginal hand out of position.

I am on the button with Queen-Jack offsuit, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. I will make a standard opening raise to 300 to exert pressure on the blinds and take advantage of my position for the rest of the hand.

I am dealt 2h 5s in the small blind, which is one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from the Button, the most profitable and standard play is to fold immediately. Playing this hand out of position would be highly detrimental.

Holding 5d 2s in the big blind is extremely weak. There is no tactical reason to call an open raise from the button with such a poor hand out of position. The risk-to-reward ratio for calling is unfavorable, and folding allows me to preserve my stack for better opportunities.
K4s is a reasonable steal candidate from the button in a 3-handed game. Opening with a standard raise puts pressure on the blinds and gives me the initiative going into the flop.

Ten-four offsuit is a very weak hand that lacks playability. Defending from the small blind out of position against a button raise with such a marginal holding is poor strategy. Folding is the most profitable play.

I am in the big blind with Jack-Nine offsuit. Pro (the button) has raised to 250. I have already committed 100 as the big blind, so it costs me 150 more to call into a total pot of 400. This gives me pot odds of approximately 2.6:1. J9o is a reasonable hand to defend the big blind with against a button raise, especially since I will be closing the pre-flop action and have a healthy stack. Calling allows me to see a flop and realize my equity without over-committing to a marginal hand.

The flop is extremely dangerous with three hearts (Ah Kh Th). I have Jc 9s, giving me a gutshot straight draw to the nut straight (a Queen), but because the board is monotone, any heart held by my opponent already makes a flush. As the first to act against the pre-flop raiser on such a coordinated board, the standard play is to check and see how the opponent reacts. I have very little equity here if a flush or a higher straight draw is present.

The board is Ah Kh Th, which is heavily connected and monotone in hearts. We hold middle pair with Ks4s and no heart draw, meaning our equity against a calling range is very poor. Betting here would likely only get called by better hands, such as an Ace, a flush, a straight, or a strong heart draw. Checking behind allows us to pot control, keep our opponent's weaker hands in the mix, and see a free turn card without risking a check-raise.

On a board of Ah-Kh-Th-Qs, I have made a straight (A-K-Q-J-T) with my Jack of Clubs. While the board has three hearts, meaning any opponent with two hearts has a flush, the button's (Pro) check on the flop suggests they likely don't have a made flush or are playing very cautiously. My straight is the best possible hand for now unless someone holds two hearts. I will bet to extract value from top-pair hands like Ax or Kx and to charge any single-heart hands that might be drawing to a flush on the river. A bet of 300 (slightly over half-pot) is appropriate to build the pot while not being so large as to scare off weaker made hands.

The board is incredibly coordinated, featuring three hearts and four to a Broadway straight. I only hold second pair with a weak kicker and absolutely no draw to improve. Flash has shown aggression on the turn, and against any reasonable betting range, I am drawing nearly dead. This is a trivial fold.
K6o is a weak hand that does not play well post-flop, even when holding the button. It is a marginal hand that I prefer to fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.
Folded to me in the Small Blind with As2s. This is a standard opening hand in a 3-handed game. I will raise to 3x the big blind to put pressure on the Big Blind and take the initiative.
K5o is a very weak hand. Even though we will have position post-flop against the Small Blind, calling a 3x raise with this holding is not profitable. Folding is the standard and safest play.

I am on the button with Qc 4c in a three-handed game. A suited Queen is a standard opening hand from this position to apply pressure on the blinds. My stack is healthy, and a 3x raise is a standard play to take control of the pot early or win it pre-flop.

Facing a 3x open from the Button, J9s is a speculative hand that plays poorly out of position. While 3-betting as a semi-bluff could be an option against a wide button opening range, folding is the most standard and lowest variance play from the Small Blind to preserve my stack.

KJo is a strong hand to defend from the Big Blind against a standard button open. Given the pot odds (200 to call into a 450 pot), a call is a mathematically sound and disciplined play to see a flop and continue my strategy.

I am in the Big Blind and flopped top pair (King) with a Jack kicker on a relatively dry, rainbow board. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Flash), the standard and most strategic play is to check, allowing me to gauge my opponent's intent through their subsequent action.

The flop 3d-Kc-5d is very favorable for my range as the pre-flop aggressor. A King is firmly in my button opening range, and Flashlight's check provides an opportunity to continuation bet and take the pot down immediately. I'll bet roughly half the pot to maintain pressure.

I have top pair with a King and a Jack kicker on a dry, rainbow board (3d-Kc-5d). My hand is very strong in this spot, and the opponent's continuation bet is standard. I choose to call rather than raise to keep the opponent's bluffing range wide and to control the pot size, allowing me to re-evaluate on the turn.

I am in the Big Blind with top pair, King-Jack, on a board of 3d-Kc-5d-4s. The board is now fairly connected, with potential straight draws (2-6). Since I am out of position, checking is the most strategic play to control the pot size and see how my opponent reacts before committing more chips. My hand is strong enough to call reasonable bets but not strong enough to lead out, given the straight potential on the board.

I have improved to a pair of 4s on the turn. While it is a marginal hand, checking behind here is the best play for pot control. Flashlight's check-call on the flop suggests they have some piece of the board or a draw (like diamonds). By checking, I realize my showdown value and avoid being check-raised off a hand that might still be best or has equity to improve.

The board texture is 3d-Kc-5d-4s-6h, which completes a straight for any player holding a 2 or a 7. My hand is a pair of Kings (Kd), which is no longer the nuts or even particularly strong given the potential straight. Since Flash checked the turn, they are either trapping with a straight or, more likely, have a weak or marginal hand that they are trying to get to showdown. Betting my pair of Kings would only serve to fold out worse hands (bluffs) while getting called or raised by better hands (straight, sets, two pairs). Checking is the most logical play to control the pot and reach a showdown as cheaply as possible.

I have a pair of 4s on a board of 3d-Kc-5d-4s-6h. Flashlight has checked the river after check-calling the flop and checking the turn. While my pair of 4s is weak, it has some showdown value against missed draws (like diamonds) or air. Betting here is unlikely to induce a fold from a better hand (like a 5, 6, or King) and only risks getting raised. Checking behind allows me to realize my showdown value and reach the end of the hand without further investment.

I am on the button with 3s 6s. This is a very weak, suited two-gapper hand. It doesn't have enough equity or playability to justify a raise or call, especially with two players behind me who could wake up with stronger hands. The optimal play is to fold and preserve my stack.

With Ace-Queen suited in the small blind and the button having folded, I am in a heads-up situation against the big blind. This is a premium hand that warrants a raise to gain the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop or build value against a weaker range.

I am in the Big Blind with 83 suited. The Small Blind has raised to 3x. I am getting 2:1 pot odds to call and I will have position post-flop (since the Button folded). While 83s is a weak hand, defending against a wide SB opening range is standard in this format, and the suited nature of the cards provides some post-flop playability.

I have top pair, top kicker with Ace-Queen of spades on a relatively dry board of 5-2-Q with two clubs. As the pre-flop aggressor, I have the initiative and this is a classic spot to continuation bet for value and protection against potential draws. Betting half-pot (300 chips) allows me to define my opponent's range and continue building the pot while my hand remains very strong.

I have 8-high with no significant draws on a Queen-high board. Flashlight raised pre-flop and is now following up with a continuation bet of half the pot. Since I have no showdown value and very little equity to improve beyond a weak backdoor flush draw, folding is the most profitable play in the long run.

I am on the button with Queen-Eight offsuit. While not a premium hand, it is perfectly acceptable for a button open in a three-handed game to attempt to steal the blinds. I will make a standard raise to 250 to see if I can take down the pot pre-flop or navigate the post-flop play with positional advantage.

I am in the Small Blind with 48o, which is a very weak starting hand. Flashlight opened from the Button with a raise to 250. While the price to call is only 200 more, playing 48o out of position against a button opener and the Big Blind still to act is a losing proposition long-term. I will fold to preserve my stack for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with 8d 2d, a very weak hand. The Button raised to 2.5x the big blind. Even though I'm getting somewhat decent odds to call, 82s plays terribly post-flop and doesn't hit enough strong draws or made hands to justify defending. Folding is the only profitable choice.

I am on the button with King-Eight of Diamonds, which is a strong starting hand in 3-handed play. Standard professional strategy dictates opening with a raise here to take control of the pot and put pressure on the blinds. A 3x big blind raise to 300 is a consistent sizing based on previous hands.

I am in the Small Blind with 5h 3c, which is a very weak offsuit connector. The player on the Button (Flash) has raised to 300, showing strength. Playing this trash hand out of position against a pre-flop raiser is a losing play. The optimal and only reasonable decision is to fold.

My hand, Ten-Six offsuit, is too weak to defend profitably against a button open of three times the big blind. Being out of position with marginal connectors makes it difficult to realize equity post-flop, especially against an aggressive opponent like Flash. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack.

Queen-Nine offsuit is a marginal hand from the Button in a 3-handed game. While it could be an open against very tight blinds, it can easily be dominated by hands in the blinds' calling and 3-betting ranges (such as A9, K9, QT, QJ, KQ, AQ). I prefer to maintain a slightly tighter opening range and fold here to preserve my stack for more profitable spots with better playability.

Queen-deuce suited is a weak starting hand to play from the small blind against the big blind. Being out of position for the rest of the hand makes it difficult to play profitably post-flop, so I will fold to preserve my chip stack.

I am on the button with Ace-Trey offsuit in a 3-handed game. This is a strong enough hand to open from the button to pick up the blinds or take the lead in the hand. A raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard opening size to apply pressure and utilize my positional advantage.

I am in the Small Blind holding Queen-Eight offsuit. The Button (Flashlight) has opened with a 3x raise. While Flashlight has been active, Queen-Eight offsuit is too weak to defend from the Small Blind out of position. Calling would likely put me in a difficult spot post-flop, and raising is too risky against an unknown range from the button. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

King-Jack suited is a strong and highly playable hand, especially heads-up against a Button open. Calling the raise keeps Flashlight's entire opening range in the pot, allows us to see a flop, and avoids inflating the pot out of position against potentially stronger hands that would call or 4-bet.

We missed the flop completely, holding only two overcards to the board with a backdoor flush draw. The standard and optimal play when defending the big blind is to check the flop to the pre-flop raiser. This allows us to evaluate Flashlight's action and easily fold if faced with a continuation bet.

The flop is 6-6-5 with two clubs. As the pre-flop aggressor, I have Ace-high with a backdoor flush draw. This board is excellent for the big blind's calling range, which likely contains many small pocket pairs, suited connectors, and sixes. Betting here as a continuation bet is risky because the board hits my opponent's range hard, and I have minimal equity. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity, and potentially see a free turn card.

With King-Jack high and no draws on the turn, our hand lacks the necessary equity to profitably semi-bluff. The 9s doesn't improve our hand. Checking is the safest and most standard option. If Flashlight bets, we can comfortably fold. If they check back again, we may realize our equity for free and potentially hit a King or Jack on the river.

The board is 6c 6d 5c 9s. My hand, Ace-Trey, is currently just Ace-high with no pair and no strong draw, as the club draw has not completed and the board is paired. Pro has checked to me twice, which could indicate a weak hand or a slow-played trap. Betting here with no equity would be a pure bluff and risky, as it is unlikely to fold out a better hand that is already willing to check. Checking back allows me to maintain pot control, avoid a potential check-raise, and potentially see the river for free to determine if my Ace-high has any showdown value.

With King-Jack high, our hand has minimal showdown value, often losing to Ace-high which makes up a large portion of Flashlight's check-down range. Since Flashlight showed weakness by checking both the flop and the turn, this is a prime opportunity to turn our hand into a bluff. The Queen of clubs on the river completes the flush draw and adds an overcard, making it an excellent scare card. A bet of slightly more than half the pot should generate enough fold equity to force out Ace-high and weak pocket pairs, allowing us to steal a pot we would otherwise likely lose at showdown.

The opponent has checked the flop and turn and is now leading the river for 400 into a 650 pot. My hand, Ace-high, is essentially a bluff-catcher that loses to any pair on the board. While the bet is relatively small, the opponent's range in the Big Blind that checks the flop and turn can still easily contain pairs or missed draws that have realized they need to bet for value or protection on the river. Given that I have no pair and the board is paired, calling with only Ace-high is unlikely to be profitable against the opponent's betting range. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button in a three-handed game with T5s. While this hand is on the weaker side of an opening range, it is suited and functional. I have been raising frequently from the button in previous hands (Hands 11, 13, 14, and 17) and have successfully picked up the blinds or controlled the pot. I will continue this aggressive strategy and raise to 3x the big blind to put pressure on the blinds.
A2o is too weak to call out of position against a 3x button open. It plays poorly post-flop and is easily dominated. Folding is the standard and safest play to preserve our stack.

I am in the big blind with Queen-three offsuit, which is a very weak hand with poor playability. Defending against a button raise with such a marginal holding is generally unprofitable. Given my stack size and the early stage of the tournament, it is disciplined to fold and conserve chips for a stronger starting hand.
3h 7d is an extremely weak offsuit hand. From the button, there's no reason to play this trash hand, so folding is the standard and optimal choice.

The button has folded, creating a heads-up situation against the big blind. Ace-high (A7o) is a solid hand in this position, giving me a strong chance to either win the pot immediately with a raise or take the initiative into the post-flop. A raise to 300 is a standard size to put pressure on the big blind.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Eight offsuit. The Small Blind (Flashlight) has raised to 300. I need to put in 200 more to call into a pot that will be 600, giving me 2:1 odds. Given that Flashlight has shown a tendency to raise frequently from the Small Blind in previous hands (Hands 9, 15, 19), my hand is strong enough to defend in this spot. I will call and see a flop.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and am acting first out of position. The board [4h, 9h, Kc] is relatively dry, although it hits a king-heavy range. With Ace-high and a backdoor heart flush draw, I have sufficient equity to make a continuation bet to maintain initiative and potentially take down the pot. A bet of 300 (half pot) is a standard sizing to represent strength and force folds from lower pairs or air.

On a board of 4h-9h-Kc, I have Jack-high with no significant draws or equity. Flashlight (the Small Blind) has made a continuation bet of 300 into a 900 pot after raising pre-flop. While the price is relatively cheap (calling 300 to win 1200), my hand is too weak to continue. I have no pair and no real backdoor potential to improve. Following a disciplined strategy, I will fold and wait for a better spot to engage.

On the river 9s, I have improved to three-of-a-kind (nines) with a King kicker. The board is Jc-2c-Qs-9h-9s. I have a King-high straight on a paired board (J-2-Q-9-9). The opponent checked the river to me. On the river 9s, the board completes as Jc-2c-Qs-9h-9s. I have three-of-a-kind (nines) with a King kicker. The pre-flop aggressor (Pro) checked the flop but bet the turn and now the river. While I have a strong hand, raising might only get called by hands that have me beaten (the aforementioned straights or boats) or force out his bluffs. By calling, I capture value from his bluffs and his thinner value bets with Qx or Jx, while minimizing my losses if he indeed has a straight. Given the pot odds and the board runout, a call is the most profitable and safest choice.
